n: Zaouia of Sidi Mohammed ben Aissa
hook: Spiritual home of the Aissawa, the brotherhood of ecstatic trance
facts: Tomb of Sheikh al-Kamil Mohamed al-Hadi ben Issa (1465-1526), founder of the Isawiyya Sufi order
Present structure rebuilt around the saint's tomb in 1776 by Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah
The annual moussem at Mouloud draws followers from across North Africa for music and processions
The order traditionally known for ecstatic dance, ghaita and percussion
legend: Tradition holds that the saint's blessing once let starving disciples eat scorpions and snakes unharmed, the origin of the brotherhood's notorious trance rites
w: Burial place of the saint who founded the Aissawa, the brotherhood whose moussem at the Prophet's birthday fills these streets with ghaita, drums and trance. Legend says his blessing let starving disciples swallow scorpions and snakes unharmed. Rebuilt over the tomb in 1776. Ecstasy as inheritance.

hook: The sultan's old parade ground, now a public square of storytellers
facts: Originally the main mechouar of Moulay Ismail's palace, used for military parades and ceremonies
Left as open space to separate the imperial palace from the city
The adjoining Lalla Aouda Mosque rebuilt 1672-1678 as the first mosque of the new capital
Now hosts evening storytellers, acrobats and musicians
w: Once the mechouar where Moulay Ismail reviewed his troops, off-limits to ordinary citizens, this vast square sat as a buffer between palace and town. The Marinid-founded mosque on its edge became the imperial capital's first. Now storytellers and acrobats work the dusk crowd. Power's parade ground, gone civilian.

hook: First and grandest of Ismail's palaces, now houses built into its walls
facts: Built 1672-1679 as the private palace of Moulay Ismail and his family, the oldest in the kasbah
Once spread over 13.5 hectares with courtyards, hammams, kitchens and gardens
Now largely ruined, with a residential neighbourhood grown among and into the walls
Vaulted passages and bastion towers still visible among the modern dwellings
w: Moulay Ismail's first great palace, finished in 1679 across thirteen hectares of courts, baths and gardens. Today families live inside the ruins, doorways and laundry lines punched through the sultan's collapsing walls. Vaulted passages and bastions surface between modern houses. An empire repurposed as a neighbourhood.

hook: The minaret that fell on its worshippers in 2010
facts: Completed 1709, commissioned by Khnata bent Bakkar, vizier under Moulay Ismail and an early female minister
Its rammed-earth minaret was the tallest in the city from Ismail's reign
On 19 February 2010 the minaret collapsed during Friday prayers, killing 41 and injuring 75
Rebuilt to its original form on King Mohammed VI's orders
w: Khnata bent Bakkar, one of Morocco's first female ministers, raised this mosque in 1709 with the tallest minaret of Ismail's reign. In February 2010 that rammed-earth tower came down mid-sermon during Friday prayers, killing forty-one. Rebuilt to its old shape by royal order. Faith and rain and tragedy.

hook: A surviving synagogue in the once-teeming Jewish quarter
facts: The Meknes mellah was founded in 1679, the third in Morocco after Fez and Marrakesh
Meknes counted over 10,000 Jews in a 1936 census; fewer than 200 remained in the mellah by the 1980s
A new mellah with several synagogues and a large school was built alongside the old in the 1920s
Still standing in the warren of lanes around Qobbat Souk
w: Moulay Ismail walled off this quarter for the Jews in 1679, third of Morocco's mellahs. By 1936 the census counted over ten thousand souls here; by the 1980s under two hundred remained, the rest gone to Casablanca, France, Israel. A synagogue endures in the lanes off Qobbat Souk. A community measured by its fading.

hook: 1938 Art Deco picture house, the last cinema standing in Meknes
facts: Opened 1938, built by Cosme Sandeaux between February and December of that year
Red-and-white Art Deco facade; a stairwell fresco by painter Marcel Couderc dates from the same year
Bought by the Tazi family shortly after independence in 1956
Restored in 2023; now the only working cinema in the city
w: Red-and-white Art Deco lines from 1938, thrown up in ten months by a Greek-born builder, with a strange Couderc fresco climbing the stairwell. Generations of Meknassi courted and dreamed in its dark. Restored in 2023, it now stands as the last working cinema in town. The single surviving screen.

hook: A protectorate-era Catholic church among the Art Deco avenues
facts: Catholic church on Avenue Mohammed V in the colonial Ville Nouvelle, part of the Archdiocese of Rabat
The new city was laid out from 1916 on Henri Prost's plan with Art Deco and Mauresque buildings
Named for the olive groves of the surrounding plain
Still active for the small remaining Christian community
w: A French church marooned among the Art Deco avenues Henri Prost drew up after 1916, named for the olive groves of the plain. The settlers who filled its pews are long gone; a small congregation keeps it open. Concrete piety from the protectorate, still ringing its bell. Empire's leftover faith.

hook: The 'new gate' opening to the old instrument market
facts: Monumental gate built as an arch within an arch, flanked by two square towers
Stands at the end of Sekkakine street on the mellah edge of the medina
Once led to the old market for musical instruments
Today fronts a congested run of cloth and electronics stalls
w: Despite the name meaning 'new gate', it has stood here for generations, an arch nested inside an arch between two squat towers at the foot of Sekkakine street. Beyond it the lane once sold lutes and drums; now cloth and phone chargers crowd the stalls. A doorway between the mellah and the noise.
